scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: Accessibility tools directory. [pause] A practical reference — what each one does, who it helps, and how to use it well.

scenes.name: Categories
scenes.text: Three categories. [pause] General-purpose AI tools. Specialised accessibility tools. And supporting tools that complement the AI ones. [pause] No single tool does everything.

scenes.name: General
scenes.text: The general-purpose tools are the most versatile. [pause] ChatGPT. Claude. Microsoft Copilot. [pause] They simplify text, translate, analyse images, speak, listen — across every accessibility area.

scenes.name: Specialised
scenes.text: Specialised tools go deeper. [pause] Otter dot AI for transcription. Microsoft Immersive Reader and Read and Write for reading support. Goblin Tools for task decomposition. [pause] Each does one thing extremely well.

scenes.name: Pictograms
scenes.text: For students with intellectual disabilities, specialised libraries matter. [pause] ARASAAC offers a free, open library of pictograms. Choiceworks supports visual routines. [pause] AI structures the text. Pictograms make it visible.

scenes.name: Tool cloud
scenes.text: The full inventory at a glance. [pause] ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, Otter, Immersive Reader, Read and Write, Goblin Tools, ARASAAC, Choiceworks. [pause] More than a teacher needs. Less than the landscape contains.

scenes.name: Selection
scenes.text: How to choose. [pause] Start with one general-purpose AI tool. Add one specialised tool matched to the student's primary need. [pause] Too many tools at once creates its own cognitive overload.

scenes.name: Considerations
scenes.text: Practical considerations apply across all of them. [pause] Cost. Privacy. Device compatibility. [pause] And — more than tool selection — training and routine. Predictability is itself an accessibility support.
